docs.title: Home
docs.text:
Message from the Author

Welcome to sheets.

Lua is powerful, lightweight, and fast—but its ecosystem still lags behind modern developer needs. This project is my first contribution toward changing that. I’m here to build serious tools that push Lua beyond scripting into real engineering.

— Shivam Sharma

docs.title: sheets
docs.text:
Fast, Production-Ready CSV Processing for Lua

sheets is a high-performance, memory-efficient CSV and tabular data toolkit designed for production workloads. Built on top of the battle-tested libcsv C library, it combines native performance with an intuitive Python-inspired API.

Whether you're building data pipelines, ETL systems, analytics platforms, or processing large datasets in Lua, sheets provides a robust, efficient solution for structured tabular data.

docs.title: Why sheets?
docs.text:
The Lua ecosystem has several CSV libraries, but most fall into one of three categories:

Minimal ParsersPure Lua LibrariesIncomplete Solutions

Limited features, simple implementations, but lacking essential functionality for production systems

Flexible and easy to modify, but significantly slower when processing large datasets

Missing modern data processing APIs or lacking consistent, intuitive interfaces

sheets solves all of these problems by combining:

  • Native C performance via libcsv for parsing and writing
  • Pythonic API inspired by Python's standard csv module
  • Complete feature set for real-world data workflows
  • Production-ready reliability and comprehensive testing

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• High Performance — Parse and write CSV files with C-level speed...
  • Memory Efficient — Streaming architecture and optimized buffering...
  • Pythonic API — Familiar interfaces like Reader, Writer...
  • Flexible Dialects — Support for different CSV formats...
  • Cross-Platform — Works seamlessly on Linux, macOS, and Windows...
  • Production-Ready — Battle-tested on large datasets...

docs.title: Quick Comparison
docs.text: Feature sheets Python csv Pure Lua Libs Parse Speed Native C Native C Script Memory Usage 644 MB (1M rows) 1261 MB (1M rows) Variable Python-style API Yes Yes Partial DictReader/DictWriter Yes Yes Limited Dialect Support Auto-detect Auto-detect No Production Ready Yes Yes Limited

docs.title: Use Cases
docs.text:
sheets is ideal for:

Data PipelinesAnalytics PlatformsBackend ServicesData PreprocessingReporting Systems

ETL workflows that require fast, reliable CSV ingestion and transformation

Processing large datasets for real-time or batch analytics

CSV handling in web applications, APIs, and data services

Preparing datasets for machine learning pipelines

Generating and parsing CSV reports at scale

docs.title: 1. Installation
docs.text:
luarocks install sheets

Or build from source. See Installation for detailed instructions.

docs.title: 2. Basic Reading
docs.text:
local csv = require("csv")

-- Simple row iteration
for row in csv.reader(io.open("data.csv")) do
    print(row[1], row[2], row[3])
end

docs.title: 3. Basic Writing
docs.text:
local csv = require("csv")

local f = io.open("output.csv", "w")
local writer = csv.writer(f)

writer:writerow({"name", "age", "city"})
writer:writerow({"Alice", 28, "New York"})
writer:writerow({"Bob", 35, "San Francisco"})

f:close()

docs.title: 4. Dictionary-Based Access
docs.text:
-- Access rows as dictionaries with header mapping
for record in csv.DictReader(io.open("data.csv")) do
    print(record.name, record.age)
end

docs.title: Performance at Scale
docs.text:
Tested on a 1 million row, 20 column CSV file with mixed data types:

Metric sheets Python csv Ratio Parse Time 9.2s 6.7s 1.4× Memory Usage 644 MB 1261 MB 0.51× Write Time 0.17s 0.59s 3.5×

Key Takeaways:

  • 2× better memory efficiency than Python
  • 3.5× faster writing performance
  • Competitive parsing with full C implementation backend
  • Scales efficiently to multi-million row

See Benchmarks for detailed performance analysis and additional test cases.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
sheets uses a hybrid Lua + C architecture for optimal performance and usability:

┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│   Lua Layer (High-Level API)        │
│  Reader, Writer, DictReader, etc.   │
└────────────┬────────────────────────┘
             │
┌────────────▼────────────────────────┐
│   C Layer (Libcsv Bindings)         │
│  Fast parsing, writing, buffering   │
└────────────┬────────────────────────┘
             │
┌────────────▼────────────────────────┐
│   libcsv Backend                    │
│  Battle-tested, standards-compliant │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘

This design ensures:

  • Lua flexibility for API design and ease of use
  • C performance for I/O-bound operations
  • Modularity for easy extension and maintenance

See Design_Overview for deep technical details.

docs.title: Core API Overview
docs.text:
ReaderWriterDictReaderDictWriter 
local csv = require("csv")

-- Stream rows as arrays
for row in csv.reader(file) do
    print(#row, row[1])
end

local csv = require("csv")

local writer = csv.writer(file)
writer:writerow({"col1", "col2"})
writer:writerows({{1, 2}, {3, 4}})

local csv = require("csv")

-- Maps rows to dictionaries with headers
for record in csv.DictReader(file) do
    print(record.email, record.name)
end

local csv = require("csv")

local writer = csv.DictWriter(file, {"name", "age"})
writer:writeheader()
writer:writerow({name="Alice", age=28})

See api_red for complete documentation of all functions and parameters.

docs.title: Documentation Structure
docs.text:
This documentation is organized into several sections:

📖 Documentation Guide

  • Installation — Setup, build from source, platform-specific notes

  • Quickstart — Hands-on tutorial with common patterns and examples

  • api_ref — Complete API documentation with parameters, return values, and examples

  • Architecture — Internal design, internals, and contribution guidelines

  • Benchmarks — Performance data, comparison with other libraries, profiling results

docs.title: Project Philosophy
docs.text:
sheets is built on three core principles:

Performance

Native C implementation for I/O-bound operations. Stream processing architecture to minimize memory footprint.

Usability

Pythonic API familiar to developers across ecosystems. Clear, predictable behavior with sensible defaults.

Reliability

Production-tested on large real-world datasets. Comprehensive error handling and consistent cross-platform behavior.

docs.text:
sheets v2.0.0

Licensed under the MIT License. See LICENSE file for details.

docs.title: Get Started Now
docs.text:
Ready to start? Head over to the Installation guide to get sheets set up.

Already familiar with CSV libraries? Jump straight to the api_ref for complete documentation.

Have questions? Check out the Quickstart for common patterns and best practices.

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text:
This document provides the complete API reference for sheets.

Import the library:

local csv = require("csv")

docs.title: Core Functions
docs.text: These are the primary one-shot APIs.

docs.title: csv.read_csv(filename, opts)
docs.text: Reads and parses an entire CSV file.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Description filename string Path to CSV file opts table (optional) Parse options

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
rows

or

nil, err

docs.title: Example
docs.text: local rows = csv.read_csv("data.csv")

docs.title: csv.parse(str, opts)
docs.text: Parse CSV data from a string.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Description str string CSV text opts table (optional) Parse options

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: rows

docs.title: Example
docs.text: local rows = csv.parse("name,age\nAlice,30")

docs.title: csv.write(rows, opts)
docs.text: Serialize rows into CSV text.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type Description rows table Array of rows opts table (optional) Write options

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: string

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
local output = csv.write({
    {"name", "age"},
    {"Alice", 30}
})

docs.title: csv.write_csv(filename, rows, opts)
docs.text: Write rows directly to a CSV file.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type filename string rows table opts table (optional)

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
true

or

nil, err

docs.title: csv.read_dicts(filename, opts)
docs.text: Read CSV directly as dictionaries.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: local rows = csv.read_dicts("data.csv")

docs.title: Reader API
docs.text:
Create a reader:

local reader = csv.reader(source, opts)

docs.title: reader:next_row()
docs.text: Returns next row.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text:
row

or

nil

docs.title: reader:readall()
docs.text: Read all rows.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: table

docs.title: reader:get(index)
docs.text: Get row by index.

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Name Type index number

docs.title: reader:count()
docs.text: Return total row count.

docs.title: reader:reset()
docs.text: Reset reader position.

docs.title: reader:column(index)
docs.text: Extract column.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: local names = reader:column(1)

docs.title: reader:filter(predicate)
docs.text: Filter rows.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
local filtered = reader:filter(function(row)
    return row[1] == "Alice"
end)

docs.title: reader:map(transformer)
docs.text: Transform rows.

docs.title: Writer API
docs.text:
Create writer:

local writer = csv.writer(file, opts)

docs.title: writer:writerow(row)
docs.text: Write single row.

docs.title: Example
docs.text: writer:writerow({"Alice", 30})

docs.title: writer:writerows(rows)
docs.text: Write multiple rows.

docs.title: DictReader API
docs.text:
Create:

local dr = csv.DictReader(source, opts)

Uses first row as headers.

docs.title: dictreader:next_row()
docs.text:
Returns next row as dictionary.

Example:

{
    name = "Alice",
    age = "30"
}

docs.title: dictreader:readall()
docs.text: Read all records.

docs.title: dictreader:reset()
docs.text: Reset cursor.

docs.title: dictreader:filter(predicate)
docs.text: Filter records.

docs.title: dictreader:map(transformer)
docs.text: Transform records.

docs.title: DictWriter API
docs.text:
Create:

local dw = csv.DictWriter(file, opts)

Required:

{
    fieldnames = {"name", "age"}
}

docs.title: dictwriter:writeheader()
docs.text: Write header row.

docs.title: dictwriter:writerow(record)
docs.text: Write dictionary row.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
dw:writerow({
    name = "Alice",
    age = 30
})

docs.title: dictwriter:writerows(records)
docs.text: Write multiple records.

docs.title: Dialects
docs.text:
Built-in dialects:

  • csv.excel
  • csv.excel_tab
  • csv.unix_dialect

docs.title: csv.register_dialect(name, config)
docs.text: Register custom dialect.

docs.title: Example
docs.text:
csv.register_dialect("pipes", {
    delim = "|"
})

docs.title: csv.get_dialect(name)
docs.text: Get dialect config.

docs.title: csv.unregister_dialect(name)
docs.text: Remove custom dialect.

docs.title: csv.list_dialects()
docs.text: Returns available dialects.

docs.title: csv.sniff(sample)
docs.text: Auto-detect CSV format.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: dialect

docs.title: csv.has_header(sample)
docs.text: Check whether sample likely contains headers.

docs.title: Returns
docs.text: boolean

docs.title: csv.filter(rows, predicate)
docs.text: Filter rows.

docs.title: csv.map(rows, transformer)
docs.text: Transform rows.

docs.title: csv.merge(rows1, rows2)
docs.text: Merge two row sets.

docs.title: csv.unique(rows, key_func)
docs.text: Remove duplicates.

docs.title: csv.sort(rows, comparator)
docs.text: Sort rows.

docs.title: csv.transpose(rows)
docs.text:
Transpose matrix.

Example:

{
    {"a", "b"},
    {"1", "2"}
}

becomes:

{
    {"a", "1"},
    {"b", "2"}
}

docs.title: csv.group_by(rows, key_func)
docs.text:
Group rows by key.

Example:

local grouped = csv.group_by(rows, function(row)
    return row[2]
end)

docs.title: csv.column(rows, index)
docs.text: Extract column values.

docs.title: Options Reference
docs.text: Most APIs accept an optional opts table.

docs.title: Parse Options
docs.text: Key Type Default delim string "," quote string " strict boolean false dialect string/table nil

docs.title: Write Options
docs.text: Key Type Default delim string "," quote string " quote_all boolean false dialect string/table nil

docs.title: Version Info
docs.text:
csv._VERSION
csv._LIBCSV_VERSION

Example:

print(csv._VERSION)
print(csv._LIBCSV_VERSION)
```# API Reference

This document provides the complete API reference for `sheets`.

Import the library:

```lua
local csv = require("csv")

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
sheets uses a layered hybrid architecture designed to balance:

  • High performance
  • Low memory usage
  • Clean API design
  • Easy extensibility

The core idea is simple:

  • Performance-critical operations are implemented in C
  • High-level APIs and utilities are implemented in Lua

This provides the speed of native code while preserving the flexibility of Lua.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
sheets consists of three main layers:

  1. User-facing API Layer
  2. Native C Core Layer
  3. Backend CSV Engine

docs.title: High-Level Flow
docs.text:
User Code
   ↓
Lua API Layer
   ↓
C Core Layer
   ↓
libcsv Backend

docs.title: Layer 1: User-Facing Lua API
docs.text:
This is the public interface exposed by:

require("csv")

This layer provides a Python-inspired API.

Modules:

csv/
├── init.lua
├── reader.lua
├── writer.lua
├── dictreader.lua
├── dictwriter.lua
├── dialect.lua
└── sniffer.lua

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
The Lua layer handles:

  • Reader API
  • Writer API
  • DictReader
  • DictWriter
  • Dialect resolution
  • CSV format detection
  • Utility functions

Examples:

csv.reader(file)
csv.writer(file)
csv.DictReader(file)
csv.sniff(text)
csv.filter(rows, fn)

This layer focuses on:

  • Ergonomics
  • API design
  • Validation
  • Convenience

docs.title: Layer 2: Native C Core
docs.text:
This layer contains performance-critical logic.

Core file:

csv/core.c

Exposed as:

require("csv.core")

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
The C layer handles:

  • CSV parsing
  • CSV serialization
  • Buffer management
  • Lua table conversion

Core functions:

core.parse_string(str, opts)
core.parse_file(path, opts)
core.write_row(row, opts)
core.write_rows(rows, opts)

docs.title: Why C?
docs.text:
CSV parsing and serialization are highly repetitive operations involving:

  • Character scanning
  • Delimiter detection
  • Quote handling
  • Memory allocation

These workloads are significantly faster in C than in pure Lua.

This design keeps:

  • Parsing fast
  • Writing fast
  • Memory overhead low

docs.title: Layer 3: Backend CSV Engine
docs.text:
The C core uses:

lib/libcsv/

Specifically:

  • csv.h
  • libcsv.c

docs.title: Backend Library
docs.text:
sheets uses libcsv, a lightweight and battle-tested C CSV parser.

Why libcsv:

  • Mature implementation
  • RFC 4180 compliant behavior
  • High performance
  • Small dependency footprint

This backend performs the low-level CSV parsing logic.

docs.title: Parsing Flow
docs.text:
Example:

local rows = csv.read_csv("data.csv")

Execution path:

csv.read_csv()
    ↓
core.parse_file()
    ↓
libcsv parser
    ↓
field callbacks
    ↓
Lua tables

Final output:

{
    {"name", "age"},
    {"Alice", "30"}
}

docs.title: Writing Flow
docs.text:
Example:

local output = csv.write(rows)

Execution path:

csv.write()
    ↓
core.write_rows()
    ↓
CSV serialization
    ↓
Output string

docs.title: Design Philosophy
docs.text: sheets was designed around several core principles.

docs.title: 1. Performance First
docs.text:
Parsing and writing are implemented in C.

This minimizes overhead in critical paths.

docs.title: 2. Lua for Flexibility
docs.text:
High-level features remain in Lua.

This makes the library:

  • Easy to modify
  • Easy to extend
  • Easy to maintain

docs.title: 3. Python-Inspired API
docs.text:
The API design takes inspiration from Python’s built-in csv module.

Examples:

  • reader
  • writer
  • DictReader
  • DictWriter
  • Sniffer

This makes the library familiar to Python users.

docs.title: 4. Minimal Dependencies
docs.text:
sheets depends only on:

  • Lua
  • libcsv

No heavy external dependencies are required.

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
sheets/
├── csv/
│   ├── core.c
│   ├── init.lua
│   ├── reader.lua
│   ├── writer.lua
│   ├── dictreader.lua
│   ├── dictwriter.lua
│   ├── dialect.lua
│   └── sniffer.lua
│
├── lib/
│   └── libcsv/
│
├── docs/
├── examples/
└── test/

docs.title: Advantages of This Architecture
docs.text:
This hybrid design provides:

  • High performance
  • Lower memory usage
  • Clean modular code
  • Easy extensibility
  • Production-ready reliability

docs.title: Future Extensions
docs.text:
The architecture is designed to support future improvements.

Potential enhancements:

  • True streaming parser
  • Async CSV reader
  • Type inference
  • Schema validation
  • Parallel parsing

The current architecture allows these features to be added without major redesign.

docs.title: Changelog - sheets
docs.text: All notable changes to sheets are documented in this file.

docs.title: New Utility Functions
docs.text:
• csv.filter(rows, predicate) - Filter rows based on predicate function
  • csv.map(rows, transformer) - Transform rows using transformer function
  • csv.merge(rows1, rows2) - Merge two CSV row sets
  • csv.unique(rows, key_func) - Remove duplicate rows
  • csv.sort(rows, comparator) - Sort rows using comparator function
  • csv.transpose(rows) - Transpose CSV matrix (rows become columns)
  • csv.group_by(rows, key_func) - Group rows by key function
  • csv.column(rows, column_index) - Extract single column from rows

docs.title: Enhanced Reader Class
docs.text:
• Reader:readall() - Get all remaining rows as array
  • Reader:get(index) - Get specific row by 1-based index
  • Reader:slice(start, finish) - Get slice of rows
  • Reader:column(col_index) - Extract single column
  • Reader:filter(predicate) - Filter rows
  • Reader:map(transformer) - Transform rows

docs.title: Enhanced DictReader Class
docs.text:
• DictReader:readall() - Get all remaining records as array
  • DictReader:count() - Get record count
  • DictReader:filter(predicate) - Filter records by predicate
  • DictReader:map(transformer) - Map records with transformer
  • BUG FIX: Fixed reset() method by properly tracking _consumed_header flag

docs.title: Enhanced DictWriter Class
docs.text: • DictWriter:writerows(dicts) - Write multiple records at once

docs.title: Documentation
docs.text:
• Professional README_COMPLETE.md with full API reference
  • CONTRIBUTING.md for developers
  • Enhanced inline code comments
  • Comprehensive test suite (test_csv_enhanced.lua)

docs.title: Testing
docs.text:
• 25 comprehensive test cases in test_csv_enhanced.lua
  • Tests for all new functions
  • Edge case coverage
  • Validation of bug fixes

docs.title: Changed
docs.text:
• Version bumped from 0.1.0 to 2.0.0
  • Enhanced error handling and validation throughout
  • Improved DictReader implementation with proper state tracking
  • Better documentation and examples

docs.title: Fixed
docs.text:
• DictReader.reset() bug: The _consumed_header flag was not being set, causing reset to fail
  • Improved null/empty field handling in DictReader
  • Enhanced error messages for invalid parameters

docs.title: Deprecated
docs.text: • None

docs.title: Removed
docs.text: • None

docs.title: Security
docs.text: • None

docs.title: Added
docs.text:
• Core CSV reader with row-based iteration
  • Core CSV writer with row-based output
  • DictReader class for dictionary-based reading
  • DictWriter class for dictionary-based writing
  • Dialect system with built-in presets (excel, excel_tab, unix)
  • Support for custom dialects
  • CSV format sniffer for auto-detection
  • Test suite with basic coverage
  • Basic examples

docs.title: Version Guide
docs.text:
• v2.0.0 (2024-07-02): Major release with new features and bug fixes - Production ready
  • v0.1.0 (2024-06-01): Initial release - Foundation version

docs.title: From 0.1.0 to 2.0.0
docs.text: No breaking changes! Your existing code will continue to work.

docs.title: New Capabilities
docs.text:
• Use new utility functions for data transformation
  • Use new Reader methods for advanced queries
  • Use new DictReader methods for filtering/mapping
  • Benefit from bug fixes in reset() functionality

docs.title: Recommended Updates
docs.text:
1. Update to v2.0.0 for bug fixes
  2. Adopt new utility functions where applicable
  3. Use new Reader/DictReader methods for cleaner code

Example of migration to new style: 

-- Old way (still works)
local rows = csv.read_csv("file.csv")
local filtered = {}
for _, row in ipairs(rows) do
    if tonumber(row[2]) > 30 then
        table.insert(filtered, row)
    end
end

-- New way (cleaner)
local rows = csv.read_csv("file.csv")
local filtered = csv.filter(rows, function(row)
    return tonumber(row[2]) > 30
end)

docs.title: Planned for v2.1.0
docs.text:
• Streaming support for large files
  • More statistical functions
  • Excel-specific features

docs.title: Planned for v3.0.0
docs.text:
• Lua coroutine-based streaming
  • Async I/O support
  • Performance optimizations

docs.title: Support
docs.text:
• For bug reports: Open an issue on GitHub
  • For feature requests: Discuss in GitHub issues
  • For help: Check README_COMPLETE.md and examples

docs.title: License
docs.text: MIT License - See LICENSE file for details

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text:
Thank you for your interest in contributing to sheets.

Contributions of all kinds are welcome.

This includes:

  • Bug reports
  • Feature requests
  • Performance improvements
  • Documentation improvements
  • Test coverage improvements
  • Code contributions

docs.title: Development Setup
docs.text:
Clone the repository:

git clone https://github.com/NNEngine/sheets.git
cd sheets

Build the native module:

./build.sh

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
sheets/
├── csv/
│   ├── core.c
│   ├── init.lua
│   ├── reader.lua
│   ├── writer.lua
│   ├── dictreader.lua
│   ├── dictwriter.lua
│   ├── dialect.lua
│   └── sniffer.lua
│
├── docs/
├── examples/
├── test/
└── lib/

docs.title: Running Tests
docs.text:
Run the test suite:

lua test/test_csv.lua

All tests should pass before submitting changes.

docs.title: Lua Code
docs.text:
• Use clear and readable code
  • Prefer small focused functions
  • Follow existing module style
  • Add comments for non-obvious logic

Example style:

local result = {}
for _, row in ipairs(rows) do
    result[#result + 1] = row
end

docs.title: C Code
docs.text:
Performance-critical logic lives in:

csv/core.c

Guidelines:

  • Keep C code minimal
  • Prioritize performance
  • Avoid unnecessary allocations
  • Document complex logic

docs.title: Contribution Workflow
docs.text:
1. Fork repository
  2. Create feature branch
  3. Make changes
  4. Add/update tests
  5. Run tests
  6. Submit pull request

Example:

git checkout -b feature/improve-parser

docs.title: Reporting Bugs
docs.text:
When reporting issues, please include:

  • Lua version
  • Operating system
  • Error message
  • Reproduction steps
  • Example CSV input (if relevant)

Example:

Lua version: 5.4
OS: Ubuntu 24.04
Issue: Parsing quoted multiline CSV fails

docs.title: Feature Requests
docs.text:
Feature requests are welcome.

Examples:

  • Streaming parser
  • Type inference
  • Schema validation
  • Async reader
  • Performance improvements

docs.title: Areas for Contribution
docs.text:
Some high-impact contribution areas:

  • Performance optimization
  • Streaming parser support
  • Improved dialect detection
  • Documentation improvements
  • Additional tests

docs.title: Code of Conduct
docs.text:
Please be respectful and constructive.

We aim to maintain a welcoming and collaborative environment.

docs.title: Questions?
docs.text:
Open an issue or start a discussion on GitHub.

Thank you for helping improve sheets.

docs.title: FAQ
docs.text: This page answers common questions about sheets.

docs.title: What is sheets?
docs.text:
sheets is a high-performance CSV and tabular data toolkit for Lua.

It combines:

  • Native C performance using libcsv
  • Python-inspired APIs
  • Lightweight tabular utilities

It is designed for:

  • CSV parsing
  • CSV writing
  • Data preprocessing
  • ETL workflows
  • Analytics pipelines

docs.title: Which Lua versions are supported?
docs.text:
sheets currently supports:

  • Lua 5.2
  • Lua 5.3
  • Lua 5.4

docs.title: Does sheets support streaming?
docs.text:
Not yet.

Currently, sheets reads the full input into memory before parsing.

Example:

local rows = csv.read_csv("data.csv")

Future versions may include:

  • True streaming parser
  • Chunk-based reading
  • Async CSV processing

docs.title: Is sheets faster than Python’s csv module?
docs.text:
It depends on the workload.

Benchmark results:

Metric sheets Python csv Parse Time ~9.2s ~6.7s Memory Usage ~644 MB ~1261 MB Write Time ~0.17s ~0.59s

Key advantages of sheets:

  • Lower memory usage
  • Faster writing performance
  • Competitive parsing speed

docs.title: Why use libcsv?
docs.text:
sheets uses libcsv because it offers:

  • High performance
  • RFC 4180 compliant behavior
  • Mature implementation
  • Minimal dependency footprint

It allows sheets to focus on API design while relying on a battle-tested parser.

docs.title: Does sheets support custom delimiters?
docs.text:
Yes.

Example:

local rows = csv.parse(text, {
    delim = ";"
})

Supported examples:

  • Comma ,
  • Semicolon ;
  • Pipe |
  • Tab \t

docs.title: Does sheets support quoted fields?
docs.text:
Yes.

Example:

name,note
Alice,"hello,world"

This is parsed correctly.

docs.title: Does sheets support multiline fields?
docs.text:
Yes.

Example:

name,note
Alice,"hello
world"

Multiline quoted fields are supported.

docs.title: Does sheets support Unicode?
docs.text:
Yes.

Example:

name,city
Shivam,東京

Unicode strings are supported as long as your Lua environment handles UTF-8 correctly.

docs.title: Can I write numbers and booleans?
docs.text:
Yes.

Values are automatically converted to strings.

Example:

csv.write({
    {"name", "active", "age"},
    {"Alice", true, 30}
})

Becomes:

name,active,age
Alice,true,30

docs.title: Why are all values strings after parsing?
docs.text:
CSV stores raw text.

Example:

age
30

Parsed result:

"30"

This is expected behavior.

If numeric conversion is needed:

local age = tonumber(row[2])

docs.title: Can I contribute?
docs.text:
Yes.

Contributions are welcome.

See the Contributing page for details.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text:
This guide explains how to install sheets using either:

  • LuaRocks (recommended)
  • Manual build from source

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
Before installing, ensure you have:

  • Lua 5.2+
  • A C compiler
  • Build tools

Supported platforms:

  • Linux
  • Windows
  • macOS (experimental)

docs.title: Install via LuaRocks (Recommended)
docs.text:
The easiest way to install sheets is through LuaRocks.

luarocks install sheets

This will automatically:

  • Build the C core (csv.core)
  • Install Lua modules
  • Make the library available system-wide

docs.title: 1. Clone Repository
docs.text:
git clone <repo-url>
cd sheets

docs.title: 2. Build Native Module
docs.text:
Run:

./build.sh

This compiles:

csv/core.so

The native module is built from:

  • csv/core.c
  • lib/libcsv/libcsv.c

docs.title: 3. Verify Build
docs.text:
After successful compilation, you should see:

csv/
├── core.so

(or core.dll on Windows)

docs.title: Windows Installation
docs.text:
You need:

  • MinGW / GCC or
  • MSVC

Example using MinGW:

gcc -O2 -shared -o csv/core.dll csv/core.c lib/libcsv/libcsv.c -Ilib/libcsv -I<lua_include_path>

Make sure your Lua installation provides:

  • lua.h
  • lauxlib.h
  • lualib.h

docs.title: Linux Installation
docs.text: Install build dependencies.

docs.title: Ubuntu / Debian
docs.text:
sudo apt install build-essential lua5.4 liblua5.4-dev luarocks

Then:

./build.sh

docs.title: Arch Linux
docs.text:
sudo pacman -S base-devel lua luarocks

Then:

./build.sh

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
Run Lua:

lua

Then:

local csv = require("csv")
print(csv._VERSION)

Expected output:

2.0.0

docs.title: Test Installation
docs.text:
Try a quick parse:

local csv = require("csv")

local rows = csv.parse("name,age\nShivam,23")

print(rows[2][1])  -- Shivam
print(rows[2][2])  -- 23

If this works, installation is successful.

docs.title: Module csv.core not found
docs.text:
This means Lua cannot find the compiled C module.

Check:

  • package.cpath
  • Build completed successfully
  • csv/core.so exists

docs.title: Missing Lua headers
docs.text:
Error:

fatal error: lua.h: No such file or directory

Install Lua development headers.

Examples:

Ubuntu:

sudo apt install liblua5.4-dev

Arch:

sudo pacman -S lua

docs.title: Permission denied on build.sh
docs.text:
Run:

chmod +x build.sh
./build.sh

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
After installation:

  • Read Quickstart for basic usage
  • Explore API Reference
  • Check Benchmarks

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text:
This guide covers the most common workflows in sheets.

By the end, you’ll know how to:

  • Read CSV files
  • Write CSV files
  • Use DictReader / DictWriter
  • Work with dialects
  • Use built-in data utilities

docs.title: Import Library
docs.text: local csv = require("csv")

docs.title: Reading CSV
docs.text:
There are two main ways to read CSV data:

  • One-shot loading (csv.read_csv)
  • Streaming via csv.reader

docs.title: Read Entire CSV File
docs.text:
local csv = require("csv")

local rows = csv.read_csv("data.csv")

for _, row in ipairs(rows) do
    print(row[1], row[2])
end

Example CSV:

name,age
Alice,30
Bob,25

Result:

{
    {"name", "age"},
    {"Alice", "30"},
    {"Bob", "25"}
}

docs.title: Parse CSV String
docs.text:
local csv = require("csv")

local text = "name,age\nAlice,30\nBob,25"
local rows = csv.parse(text)

print(rows[2][1]) -- Alice
print(rows[2][2]) -- 30

docs.title: Reader API
docs.text:
Use csv.reader() when you want a Python-style reader.

local csv = require("csv")

local f = io.open("data.csv")

for row in csv.reader(f) do
    print(row[1], row[2])
end

f:close()

docs.title: Writing CSV
docs.text:
You can either:

  • Serialize rows into a CSV string
  • Write directly to a file

docs.title: Write CSV String
docs.text:
local csv = require("csv")

local rows = {
    {"name", "age"},
    {"Alice", 30},
    {"Bob", 25}
}

local output = csv.write(rows)
print(output)

Output:

name,age
Alice,30
Bob,25

docs.title: Write CSV File
docs.text:
local csv = require("csv")

local rows = {
    {"name", "age"},
    {"Alice", 30},
    {"Bob", 25}
}

csv.write_csv("output.csv", rows)

docs.title: Writer API
docs.text:
local csv = require("csv")

local f = io.open("output.csv", "w")
local writer = csv.writer(f)

writer:writerow({"name", "age"})
writer:writerow({"Alice", 30})
writer:writerow({"Bob", 25})

f:close()

docs.title: DictReader
docs.text:
DictReader uses the first row as column headers.

Example CSV:

name,age,city
Alice,30,NYC
Bob,25,LA

docs.title: Read as Dictionaries
docs.text:
local csv = require("csv")

local f = io.open("data.csv")

for record in csv.DictReader(f) do
    print(record.name, record.age, record.city)
end

f:close()

Example record:

{
    name = "Alice",
    age = "30",
    city = "NYC"
}

docs.title: One-shot Dict Read
docs.text:
local csv = require("csv")

local records = csv.read_dicts("data.csv")

docs.title: DictWriter
docs.text:
Write dictionaries using named fields.

local csv = require("csv")

local f = io.open("output.csv", "w")

local dw = csv.DictWriter(f, {
    fieldnames = {"name", "age", "city"}
})

dw:writeheader()

dw:writerow({
    name = "Alice",
    age = 30,
    city = "NYC"
})

f:close()

docs.title: Working with Dialects
docs.text:
Dialects control CSV formatting.

Built-in dialects:

  • excel
  • excel_tab
  • unix_dialect

docs.title: Use Custom Delimiter
docs.text:
local rows = csv.parse(text, {
    delim = ";"
})

Example:

name;age
Alice;30

docs.title: Register Custom Dialect
docs.text:
local csv = require("csv")

csv.register_dialect("pipes", {
    delim = "|"
})

local rows = csv.parse("a|b|c\n1|2|3", {
    dialect = "pipes"
})

docs.title: Sniffer
docs.text:
Automatically detect CSV format.

local csv = require("csv")

local sample = "name,age\nAlice,30\nBob,25"

local dialect = csv.sniff(sample)
print(dialect.delim)

docs.title: Utility Functions
docs.text: sheets includes lightweight utilities for tabular data.

docs.title: Filter Rows
docs.text:
local adults = csv.filter(rows, function(row)
    return tonumber(row[2]) >= 18
end)

docs.title: Map Rows
docs.text:
local transformed = csv.map(rows, function(row)
    row[2] = tonumber(row[2])
    return row
end)

docs.title: Sort Rows
docs.text:
local sorted = csv.sort(rows, function(a, b)
    return tonumber(a[2]) < tonumber(b[2])
end)

docs.title: Group Rows
docs.text:
local grouped = csv.group_by(rows, function(row)
    return row[3]
end)

docs.title: Extract Column
docs.text: local names = csv.column(rows, 1)

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
You now know the basics of sheets.

Next:

  • Read Architecture to understand internals
  • Explore API Reference for complete documentation
  • Check Benchmarks for performance analysis

docs.title: Benchmarks
docs.text:
This page documents performance benchmarks for sheets.

The goal is to evaluate:

  • Parsing speed
  • Writing speed
  • Memory efficiency

and compare against Python’s built-in csv module.

docs.title: Benchmark Setup
docs.text:
Benchmarks were run using a synthetic CSV file with:

  • 1,000,000 rows
  • 20 columns per row
  • Mixed field types:
  • integers
  • floats
  • strings
  • quoted strings
  • Approximate file size: 200–300 MB

Example row structure:

id,count,price,name,quoted,city,...
1,2,0.0100,user_1,"quoted,value",city_1,...

docs.title: Test Operations
docs.text: Three main benchmarks were measured.

docs.title: 1. Parse Benchmark
docs.text:
Measures:

  • Reading CSV file
  • Parsing all rows
  • Fully loading into memory

Equivalent to:

local rows = csv.read_csv("benchmark_1m.csv")

docs.title: 2. Write Benchmark
docs.text:
Measures:

  • Serializing rows into CSV text

Equivalent to:

local output = csv.write(rows)

Repeated 100,000 times for stable measurements.

docs.title: 3. Memory Benchmark
docs.text:
Measures:

  • Memory usage before parse
  • Memory usage after full parse

This represents total in-memory footprint after loading all rows.

docs.title: Benchmark Results
docs.text: Metric sheets Python csv Parse Time ~9.2s ~6.7s Memory Usage ~644 MB ~1261 MB Write Time ~0.17s ~0.59s

docs.title: Parsing Performance
docs.text:
Library Parse Time Python csv ~6.7s sheets ~9.2s

Python’s built-in csv module parses faster due to highly optimized C internals and decades of optimization.

However, sheets remains competitive while offering excellent memory efficiency.

docs.title: Memory Efficiency
docs.text: Library Memory Usage Python csv ~1261 MB sheets ~644 MB

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
sheets uses approximately:

  • ~49% less memory
  • Nearly 2× lower memory usage

This is one of the strongest advantages of sheets.

For large datasets, lower memory usage can significantly improve stability and scalability.

docs.title: Writing Performance
docs.text: Library Write Time Python csv ~0.59s sheets ~0.17s

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
sheets achieves approximately:

  • ~3.5× faster writing performance

This makes it particularly effective for:

  • Data export pipelines
  • CSV generation workloads
  • Reporting systems

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
sheets provides:

  • Competitive parsing speed
  • Excellent memory efficiency
  • Outstanding write performance

Main strengths:

  • ~2× lower memory usage than Python csv
  • ~3.5× faster writing
  • High-performance C backend using libcsv

docs.title: Benchmark Scripts
docs.text:
Benchmark scripts are included in the repository.

Lua benchmark:

examples/comparison.lua

Python benchmark:

test/python_csv_bench.py

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
Benchmark results may vary depending on:

  • CPU
  • RAM
  • OS
  • Lua version
  • Compiler optimization flags

These benchmarks are intended to provide practical comparative insight rather than absolute performance guarantees.
